title: A drawer full of cables
content: Early AI tooling often looked like a drawer full of incompatible cables. Every AI app that wanted to reach Slack, GitHub, or a database often needed its own custom connection to each one, and every tool had to be re-integrated for every app. This created repeated engineering work on both sides.
next: Multiply apps by tools and you see how bad it gets.

title: The multiplication trap
content: Ten AI apps and twenty tools can mean two hundred custom integrations, each built, tested, and maintained separately. Every new app starts from zero, and every tool update can break several connections. This kind of N-times-M growth is a strong sign that a standard is needed.
next: The hardware world already showed the way out.

title: Enter MCP
content: The Model Context Protocol, introduced by Anthropic in late 2024 and since adopted across the industry, is an open standard for connecting AI applications to tools and data. Open means anyone can implement it - it is not locked to one company's models or products.
next: The nickname that stuck says it all.

title: USB-C for AI
content: USB-C is a useful analogy because it shows what a standard can do: one connector works across many devices and uses. MCP aims to do something similar for AI tooling. Build one MCP connector for a tool, and many MCP-capable AI apps can use it. Instead of maintaining every app-to-tool link separately, the work shifts toward reusable connectors.
next: So what does one of these connectors actually look like?

title: Servers offer, apps discover
content: On the tool side, an MCP server exposes capabilities and explains how to call them. A GitHub server might offer actions such as 'list pull requests' or 'create issue'. The AI app connects, reads that capability list, and makes it available to the model through the same tool-calling loop used elsewhere. The main difference is standardized discovery and connection.
next: Nothing new for the model to learn - just new hands to use.

title: The same loop, standardized
content: MCP does not replace tool calling - it standardizes how tools are found and connected. The model still asks, the tool still runs, the result still comes back. What changed is the plumbing: instead of custom wiring per app and per tool, one protocol carries it all. Boring plumbing, transformative effect.
next: And the effect is a whole ecosystem.

title: An ecosystem of ready-made connectors
content: Because anyone can publish an MCP server, a growing ecosystem of connectors now exists for GitHub, Slack, databases, calendars, file systems, browsers, and more. This means setting up an AI assistant increasingly looks like choosing from reusable integrations instead of building each one from scratch. Standardization helps the ecosystem scale faster.
next: There is a quiet strategic benefit too.

title: Less lock-in, more choice
content: When connectors follow a standard, they are less tightly tied to one AI vendor. If you switch assistants or providers, the same connector can often remain useful. Standards do not remove every migration cost, but they usually reduce lock-in and improve user choice.
next: You now have every building block. Time to see how real products assemble them.
